w:r.w:t: Based on "The Day the Dam Broke" by James Groves Thurber
Premium Study Resource for Students • Aligned with Curriculum Objectives
Mob mentality, panic, and the power of rumor
Civic responsibilities during crisis; critical thinking in emergencies
How would you feel if told of a mishap in your vicinity?
What civic responsibilities would you take in a panicking incident?
Connects text to real-world civic duty
"Everybody in town thought that the dam broke"
The experience both "ennobled and demoralized" the community.
: He believed Nathan Bedford Forrest's cavalry was attacking, not a flood—showing how panic distorts perception.
Part 2: The Panic Begins – A Rumor Spreads Like Fire
, High Street was calm with normal business activity.
—possibly for an innocent reason (meeting his wife).
, everyone on High Street was running east.
The mumble crystallized into the dread cry:
were abruptly in full flight, shouting
Part 3: The Chaos Unfolds – Mob Mentality in Action
flowed eastward from stores, offices, homes.
leaving fires burning, food cooking, doors wide open
and took eggs and bread—but was swept along by the crowd.
, after being stunned with an ironing board, eventually joined the cry:
, trained for quick decisions, joined the panic after hearing a child's gasp:
Part 4: Absurdity and Irony – The Panic Peaks
No one could compute exactly how many fled; the panic extended
: Even if the dam had broken, water would have risen only
East Side was never in danger
would have been needed to flood the East Side.
Part 5: Personal Accounts – Aunt Edith and Dr. Mallory
was in a movie theatre when running feet and shouts interrupted the film.
She ran with the crowd, asking a determined woman:
for rushing water and ran until collapse.
His authority lent false credibility to the panic:
he shouted—convincing others the disaster was real.
Part 6: The Panic Ends – Truth Restores Order
The next day, the city resumed business
"There are few alarms in the world more terrifying than 'The dam has broken!' there are few persons capable of stopping to reason when that clarion cry strikes upon their ears."
The panic began with one person running; within minutes, 2,000 fled—based on an unverified cry, not facts.
"The fact that we were all as safe as kittens under a cook stove did not... assuage... the fine despair... which seized upon the residents."
The narrator's mother turned out fires and packed supplies; others abandoned homes entirely—showing varied responses to crisis.
Thurber uses gentle satire: grandfather brandishing a sabre against "cavalry"; Dr. Mallory fleeing roller-skates; the lieutenant-colonel taking orders from a child.
"The dam has not broken!" announced by soldiers—initially misunderstood as confirmation of disaster
Highlights how panic distorts communication; adds satirical humor
"Black streams of people flowed eastward... shouting and screaming"
Creates vivid picture of chaotic mass movement
"He was taller than six feet and weighed almost a hundred and seventy pounds—we were passed... by practically everybody else"
Emphasizes absurdity and humor of the situation
Reference to Nathan Bedford Forrest (Confederate general); Marie Celeste (ghost ship)
Adds historical/literary depth; compares panic to mysterious abandonment
Grandfather's military delusion; Dr. Mallory's mistaken roller-skates
Gently mocks human irrationality under stress
Mimics the contagious, rhythmic nature of panic; builds tension
Q1: What and where did the panic start?

. It began when one person suddenly started running east on Broad Street—possibly for an innocent reason like meeting his wife. Within ten minutes, everyone from the Union Depot to the Courthouse was running, and the cry
Q2: In what ways does the text illustrate the theme of mob mentality?
The text shows mob mentality through: (1)
—"few persons capable of stopping to reason" when the cry struck; (3)
—a lieutenant-colonel took cues from a child; (5)
—everyone believed the dam broke despite no evidence.
Q3: How did different individuals and groups react to this news?
: Initially thought cavalry was attacking; brandished sabre; later joined the cry.
: Turned out fires, packed eggs/bread, planned refuge in Memorial Hall—but was swept along.
: Ran with crowd, asked questions but got no clear answer.
: Mistook roller-skates for water; ran until collapse, lending false authority to panic.
: Trained for quick decisions, immediately joined panic after hearing child.
: Eventually restored order by announcing truth via megaphones.
Q4: How did the panic end so abruptly and why did nobody talk about it for a long time?
militiamen rode through in motor lorries
Though this initially increased confusion, truth eventually dispelled fear. People didn't joke about it afterward because the experience was
—they had acted irrationally, abandoned responsibilities, and fled from a non-existent threat. As the text states:
"It was two years or more before you dared treat the breaking of the dam lightly."
Q5: How long did the panic last? How was order restored?
not more than two hours in all
This official confirmation gradually calmed the crowds, though it initially caused more confusion when some misheard the message.
Q6: How does the author describe the business and behavior of people in Columbus the next day?
"The next day, the city went about its business as if nothing had happened, but there was no joking."
People resumed normal activities but avoided mentioning the incident. The silence reflected shame and the lingering emotional impact of having succumbed to irrational panic.
Q7: Describe the response of law enforcement, fire-fighters, and military personnel during the panic. How did their actions demonstrate civic duty?
Initially, even trained personnel joined the panic (e.g., the lieutenant-colonel). However,
by organizing motor lorries, using megaphones to spread the truth, and patrolling to restore calm. Their actions show that civic responsibility includes
providing accurate information and maintaining order
during crises—even when the crisis is based on rumor.
Q8: Amidst the chaos, how did the author's family uphold civic responsibilities?
The narrator's mother demonstrated responsibility by
(eggs, bread). Though swept along by the crowd, her actions show awareness of community safety (preventing fires) and preparedness. This contrasts with those who abandoned homes entirely.
1. The flood in Columbus, Ohio, in 1913 was real and caused extensive damage on the East Side.
"The East Side... had never been in any danger at all."
2. The panic started when people began running east, thinking the dam had broken.
"Suddenly somebody began to run... 'The dam has broke!'"
3. Aunt Edith was in a grocery store when she first heard about the supposed dam break.
She was in a movie theatre on High Street.
4. When the rumor spread, people ran in all directions away from the dam.
5. The police and firemen tried to calm people down by saying the dam had not broken.
Militiamen (not police/firemen) eventually announced the truth via megaphones.
6. Even if the dam had broken, the water level would not have risen more than two additional inches on the West Side.
"City engineers pointed out that even if the dam had broken, the water level would not have risen more than two additional inches in the West Side."
7. Dr. Mallory mistook the swishing of roller-skates for the sound of rushing water.
"Dr. Mallory mistook the swishing of skates for the sound of rushing water."
8. The panic lasted for more than five hours.
"The shouting, weeping, tangled evacuation of the city lasted not more than two hours in all."
9. People joked about the incident the next day.
"The next day, the city went about its business as if nothing had happened, but there was no joking."
10. The narrator's mother took eggs and bread when fleeing.
"I remember... that my mother... took with her a dozen eggs and two loaves bread."
1. The rumor "The dam has broke!" triggered:
B) Assembly of mob on roads
2. The grandfather mistakenly believes that the menace the city is facing is:
3. The misconception about the dam breaking that caused panic was:
A) The East Side was in imminent danger
B) High Street would be engulfed in floodwaters
C) The water level would rise over two feet
D) The West Side was under thirty feet of water
(People feared East Side danger, but it was never at risk)
4. What did Dr. Mallory mistake for the sound of rushing water?
5. The event that finally brought order and dispelled fear after the panic was:
B) Militiamen announcing the dam had not broken
6. The author's mother took eggs and loaves of bread with her and thought to:
B) Travel to East from South
C) Take refuge in top of Memorial Hall
D) Escape with others to safer side
7. After two years, the city residents' reaction on the incident was that:
A) They laughed about it jokingly
B) They still spoke about it with fear
C) As if nothing had happened
D) They celebrated it as a historic event
(Text: "It was two years or more before you dared treat the breaking of the dam lightly")
8. What does the story reveal about human behavior in groups?
A) People become more logical in groups
B) People seek leadership and clarity
C) Panic can spread quickly without reason
D) Group behavior prevents irrational action
9. The text "The Day the Dam Broke" is ______ in nature:
10. How far did some people run during the panic?
11. What does "went through" mean in "what my family and I went through"?
A) Passed physically through a place
12. In context, what does "melted away" mean?
B) Reached the same level or position as someone ahead
14. What does "shut up like a clam" mean?
B) Fell silent or refused to talk
16. Why does Thurber include the detail that the sun shone quietly with no sign of water?
B) To emphasize the irony that panic was based on rumor, not reality
C) To suggest climate change caused the flood
D) To highlight the beauty of Columbus
17. How does the character of Dr. Mallory strengthen the theme of mob mentality?
A) He shows that even respected, rational people can be swept up in panic
B) He proves that doctors are unreliable in emergencies
C) He demonstrates the importance of medical training
D) He suggests that beards cause poor judgment
18. What lesson about civic responsibility can modern readers apply from this text?
A) Always follow the crowd in emergencies
B) Verify information before acting; maintain calm to help others think clearly
19. Which quote BEST shows the contagious nature of panic?
A) "I am having a fine time now and wish Columbus were here"
B) "Inside of ten minutes, everybody on High Street... was running"
C) "The sun shone quietly and there was nowhere any sign of oncoming waters"
D) "It was two years or more before you dared treat the breaking of the dam lightly"
20. Why is the ending—people refusing to joke about the incident—significant?
A) It shows Columbus residents lacked humor
B) It reveals the lasting shame and psychological impact of irrational group behavior
C) It proves the dam actually broke later
D) It suggests Thurber disliked his hometown
"What my family and I went through during the 1913 flood"
"Most of the others gave up, exhausted"
"My mother turned out all the fires"
To be pulled or led by others
Reached same position as someone ahead
"Dr. Mallory... passed me... He caught up with me at Ohio Avenue"
To face forward; consider the future
"She gave me a quick glance and then looked ahead again"
"Who will shut up like a clam if you mention the Afternoon of the Great Run"
"A tall spare woman... ran past me down the middle of the street"
"Groups of refugees melted away and slunk home"
"There are few alarms in the world more terrifying than 'The dam has broken!'"
Shows power of rumor to override reason
Repetition mimics contagious panic; central cry of the mob
"The fact that we were all as safe as kittens under a cook stove..."
Irony: danger existed only in perception, not reality
Captures helplessness and loss of rational inquiry in panic
Truth that restored order; highlights importance of accurate information
2,000+ flee east → Authority figures join panic → Militiamen announce tr
Lasting shame prevents joking about incident
Always support answers with specific phrases from the text.
For theme questions, focus on: mob mentality, rumor vs. fact, civic duty, irony.
When analyzing characters, note how even rational people succumb to group panic.
Use quotes to strengthen critical thinking responses.
Remember: Thurber uses humor to critique human behavior—not to mock victims.
